clause_types.name: Non-Compete Clause
clause_types.description: Restricts the employee from engaging in competing business activities during and after the termination of employment. Non-compete clauses must be carefully calibrated to be enforceable, as courts in most EU jurisdictions apply proportionality tests considering the duration, geographic scope, and breadth of restricted activities against the employer's legitimate business interest in protecting trade secrets, client relationships, and specialized training investments.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the restricted activities precisely, avoiding overbroad language such as "any business that competes" in favour of specific sectors, products, or services. Specify the geographic scope (national, regional, or limited to specific territories where the employer operates). Limit the duration to what is strictly necessary, typically 6-12 months post-termination; clauses exceeding 12 months face heightened scrutiny in most EU jurisdictions. In jurisdictions requiring compensation during the restriction period (e.g., Germany under HGB §74, France, Italy), specify the compensation amount (typically 50-100% of base salary). Address the employer's right to waive the restriction (with notice period for waiver). Consider including a garden leave mechanism as an alternative or supplement. Specify the remedies for breach (injunctive relief, liquidated damages, forfeiture of deferred compensation). Address the interaction with statutory requirements in the employee's jurisdiction.

variations.compensated-restriction: Non-compete with mandatory compensation during the restriction period, as required in Germany, France, and Italy
variations.activity-specific: Restriction limited to specific competing products, services, or client accounts rather than broad industry exclusion
variations.waivable: Employer retains discretion to waive the restriction upon termination, typically with a specified notice period

clause_types.name: Non-Solicitation Clause
clause_types.description: Prohibits the departing employee from soliciting or enticing away the employer's clients, customers, suppliers, or other employees for a specified period following termination. Non-solicitation clauses are generally more readily enforceable than non-compete clauses because they impose a narrower restriction that is more directly connected to the employer's protectable interest in its client relationships and workforce stability.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Distinguish between client non-solicitation and employee non-solicitation, as they protect different interests. For client non-solicitation, limit the restriction to clients with whom the employee had material dealings during a specified look-back period (typically 12-24 months before termination). Define "solicitation" carefully to distinguish active solicitation from passive acceptance of approaches by former clients. For employee non-solicitation, specify the categories of employees covered (direct reports, team members, or employees in the same business unit). Specify the duration (6-12 months is typical; 12 months is the common market standard). Address the interaction with non-compete restrictions to avoid duplication. Consider carve-outs for public advertising not targeted at specific individuals.

variations.client-only: Restriction limited to solicitation of clients and customers with whom the employee had personal dealings
variations.employee-only: Restriction limited to soliciting or recruiting the employer's employees, typically restricted to specific teams or seniority levels
variations.combined: Both client and employee non-solicitation with separately defined scope, duration, and remedies

compliance_refs: UK case law: Patsystems Holdings Ltd v Neilly [2012] EWHC 2609

clause_types.name: Garden Leave
clause_types.description: Permits the employer to require the employee to remain at home during the notice period while continuing to receive full pay and benefits. Garden leave serves as an alternative or complement to post-termination restrictive covenants, enabling the employer to keep the employee away from clients, colleagues, and confidential information while the employee's knowledge becomes stale. The clause must be supported by an express contractual right, as implied garden leave rights are limited under English common law.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Include an express contractual right to place the employee on garden leave during all or part of the notice period. Specify the employer's obligations during garden leave (continued salary, benefits, pension contributions, and any contractual bonuses). Define the employee's obligations during garden leave: remain available for work if required, not contact clients or colleagues, not attend competitor premises, return company property, maintain confidentiality. Address the interaction with post-termination restrictive covenants. Consider whether the garden leave period should count toward or reduce any post-termination restriction period (many courts will credit garden leave against non-compete duration). Specify the employer's right to require the employee to perform specific duties, attend handover meetings, or assist with transition during garden leave. Address the employee's right to take alternative employment during garden leave (generally prohibited). Reference the implied duty of trust and confidence and the impact of William Hill Organisation Ltd v Tucker [1999] ICR 291.

variations.full-period: Garden leave for the entire notice period with no requirement to attend the workplace
variations.partial: Garden leave for a specified portion of the notice period, with attendance required for handover activities
variations.discretionary: Employer discretion to impose garden leave at any point during the notice period

compliance_refs: UK case law: William Hill Organisation Ltd v Tucker [1999] ICR 291
UK case law: SG&R Valuation Service v Boudrais [2008] EWHC 1340

clause_types.name: TUPE Transfer Provisions
clause_types.description: Addresses the automatic transfer of employees and their terms and conditions of employment upon a relevant transfer of an undertaking or a service provision change, as required by the Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) Regulations 2006 (TUPE) implementing the EU Acquired Rights Directive 2001/23/EC. The clause allocates liabilities between the transferor and transferee for employee claims, consultation obligations, and ongoing employment costs.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Require the transferor to provide Employee Liability Information (ELI) at least 28 days before the transfer, including: identity and age of transferring employees, particulars of employment, disciplinary and grievance records from the preceding two years, details of any collective agreements, and details of any claims, actions, or orders. Include comprehensive warranties regarding the accuracy and completeness of the ELI. Allocate liability for pre-transfer employee claims (transferor) and post-transfer claims (transferee). Address the obligation to inform and consult with employee representatives or, where no representatives exist, directly affected employees. Specify indemnities for breach of information and consultation obligations. Address the treatment of occupational pension schemes (which are partially excluded from TUPE). Consider harmonisation of terms post-transfer and the restrictions on dismissal by reason of the transfer. Address measures the transferee envisages taking in respect of transferring employees and the notification requirements.

variations.asset-transfer: TUPE provisions for transfer of a business or undertaking as a going concern
variations.service-provision-change: TUPE provisions for outsourcing, insourcing, or re-tendering of service contracts
variations.cross-border: Transfer provisions addressing multiple jurisdictions under the EU Acquired Rights Directive

clause_types.name: Notice Period
clause_types.description: Specifies the minimum period of advance notice that either party must give to terminate the employment relationship. Notice periods serve both employee protection and employer operational continuity, and must comply with statutory minimums that vary by jurisdiction and length of service. In many EU jurisdictions, the statutory minimum notice period increases with the employee's length of service, and contractual provisions must meet or exceed these floors.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the notice period for both employer-initiated and employee-initiated termination (these may differ, particularly for senior roles). Ensure the contractual notice period meets or exceeds the statutory minimum in the applicable jurisdiction. Under UK law, statutory minimum notice is one week per year of service up to 12 weeks (Employment Rights Act 1996 s.86). Under German law, statutory notice periods range from four weeks to seven months depending on length of service (BGB §622). Address the form of notice (written notice to a specified address or email). Specify whether the notice period runs from the date of receipt or the end of the calendar month. Address payment in lieu of notice (PILON) and whether the employer has the contractual right to make a PILON (express PILON clause). Consider the interaction with garden leave provisions. Address what constitutes gross misconduct warranting summary dismissal without notice. Specify the treatment of benefits, bonus accrual, and equity vesting during the notice period.

variations.symmetric: Equal notice periods for employer and employee termination
variations.asymmetric: Longer notice period for employer termination than employee termination, reflecting stronger employee protection
variations.graduated: Notice period increases with length of service, mirroring or exceeding statutory requirements

clause_types.name: Probationary Period
clause_types.description: Establishes an initial period of employment during which either party may terminate the relationship with shorter notice and reduced procedural requirements, enabling the employer to assess the employee's suitability for the role and the employee to evaluate whether the position meets their expectations. Under EU Directive 2019/1152 on Transparent and Predictable Working Conditions, probationary periods must be proportionate to the nature and duration of the employment and must not exceed six months unless objectively justified.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the duration of the probation period (typically 3-6 months; EU Directive 2019/1152 limits probation to six months). Define the reduced notice period during probation (typically one week). Specify the assessment process: interim reviews, performance criteria, and confirmation of successful completion. Address the extension of probation (permitted in limited circumstances, such as employee absence during the probation period, but must remain proportionate under EU Directive 2019/1152 Art 8(2)). Specify the consequences of unsuccessful completion (termination with notice or non-renewal). Address whether unfair dismissal protections apply during probation (in the UK, the qualifying period for unfair dismissal is two years, but discrimination and whistleblowing claims have no qualifying period). Specify the employee's entitlements during probation (benefits, training, holiday accrual). Consider jurisdictions where probationary dismissal requires objective justification.

variations.standard: Three-month probation with one interim review and confirmation at conclusion
variations.extended: Six-month probation with monthly reviews and the possibility of one extension of up to three months
variations.performance-based: Probation linked to achievement of specified performance milestones rather than a fixed duration

clause_types.name: Restrictive Covenant (General)
clause_types.description: An umbrella provision encompassing all post-termination restrictions imposed on the employee, including non-compete, non-solicitation, non-dealing, and non-poaching obligations. The enforceability of restrictive covenants in employment contracts is subject to the restraint of trade doctrine, requiring the employer to demonstrate that each restriction protects a legitimate business interest and goes no further than is reasonably necessary for that protection.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Draft each restriction as a separate, severable sub-clause so that if one restriction is found unenforceable, the remaining restrictions survive. Identify the specific legitimate business interest protected by each restriction (trade secrets, confidential information, client connections, workforce stability). Apply the three-part enforceability test: (1) the restriction protects a legitimate proprietary interest, (2) the restriction is reasonable between the parties, and (3) the restriction is reasonable in the public interest. Specify the duration, geographic scope, and scope of restricted activities for each restriction separately. Include a severability clause permitting the court to blue-pencil or reduce unreasonable restrictions rather than striking the entire provision. Consider including a declaration clause where the employee acknowledges the reasonableness of the restrictions. Address the remedies available for breach (injunctive relief, damages, forfeiture provisions). Ensure compliance with local mandatory rules on compensated restrictions.

variations.comprehensive: Full suite of post-termination restrictions including non-compete, non-solicitation, non-dealing, and non-poaching
variations.tailored: Restrictions tailored to the employee's specific role, seniority, and access to protectable interests
variations.tiered: Different levels of restriction for different categories of employees based on seniority or function

compliance_refs: UK restraint of trade doctrine: Nordenfelt v Maxim Nordenfelt Guns [1894] AC 535

clause_types.name: Stock Option and Equity Vesting
clause_types.description: Governs the grant, vesting schedule, exercise conditions, and treatment upon termination of stock options, restricted stock units (RSUs), or other equity-based compensation awarded to the employee. Equity incentive provisions must address the complex interaction between employment law, securities regulation, and tax treatment across multiple jurisdictions where the employee may have rendered services.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the type of equity award (stock options, restricted stock units, performance shares, phantom equity). Define the vesting schedule: time-based (typically four years with a one-year cliff), performance-based (linked to individual or company KPIs), or hybrid. Specify the vesting commencement date and the treatment of partial vesting periods. Address acceleration provisions: single-trigger (acceleration upon change of control) or double-trigger (acceleration upon change of control plus termination). Define the treatment of unvested equity upon termination by different means (resignation, termination for cause, termination without cause, redundancy, death, disability). Specify the exercise period following termination (typically 90 days for voluntary leavers, 12 months for involuntary termination). Address clawback provisions for vested equity (link to emp-clawback). Consider the tax implications and the employer's withholding obligations. Address the interaction with settlement agreements and the treatment of equity in compromise negotiations.

variations.time-based: Standard four-year vesting with one-year cliff and monthly or quarterly vesting thereafter
variations.performance-based: Vesting contingent on achievement of specified company or individual performance targets
variations.double-trigger: Acceleration only upon both a change of control event and subsequent termination within a specified period

compliance_refs: UK Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003 Part 7
EU Shareholder Rights Directive II (2017/828) Art 9a-9b

clause_types.name: Clawback Provision
clause_types.description: Entitles the employer to recover previously paid or vested compensation (bonuses, commissions, equity awards) upon the occurrence of specified trigger events such as financial restatement, material misconduct, breach of restrictive covenants, or regulatory action. Clawback provisions are increasingly mandated by financial regulators for senior executives in regulated firms and are considered best practice in corporate governance across all sectors.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the trigger events for clawback: material financial restatement, fraud or dishonesty, gross misconduct, material breach of employment terms, breach of restrictive covenants, regulatory censure or fine. Specify the look-back period (typically 3-7 years from the date of payment or vesting). Define the types of compensation subject to clawback (annual bonus, deferred bonus, equity awards, sign-on payments). Specify the recovery mechanism: repayment demand, set-off against future compensation, cancellation of unvested awards, adjustment of future awards. Address the procedural requirements: notice to the employee, opportunity to make representations, decision-making process. Consider the interaction with local employment law restrictions on deductions from wages (UK Employment Rights Act 1996 s.13). Address the tax treatment of clawed-back compensation. For regulated financial services firms, ensure compliance with PRA/FCA Remuneration Code and EBA Guidelines on Sound Remuneration Policies.

variations.regulatory-mandated: Clawback provisions complying with financial services regulatory requirements (PRA/FCA Remuneration Code)
variations.misconduct-only: Clawback triggered only by employee fraud, dishonesty, or material misconduct
variations.performance-based: Clawback triggered by material financial restatement or failure to meet performance conditions

compliance_refs: EBA Guidelines on Sound Remuneration Policies (EBA/GL/2021/04)

clause_types.name: Bonus Deferral
clause_types.description: Requires that a proportion of the employee's variable remuneration is deferred for a specified period before vesting and payment, allowing for the application of malus (reduction of unvested awards) and clawback (recovery of vested awards) provisions. Deferral is mandatory for certain categories of staff in regulated financial services firms under the PRA/FCA Remuneration Code and EBA Guidelines, and is increasingly adopted as best practice across other sectors.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the proportion of variable remuneration subject to deferral (40-60% is typical for material risk takers in regulated firms; 60% for senior management and material risk takers with particularly high variable remuneration under EBA Guidelines). Define the deferral period (typically 3-5 years for senior management in regulated firms; 4-5 years for PRA-designated senior management functions). Specify the vesting schedule (pro rata annual or cliff vesting at the end of the deferral period). Define malus conditions: events that permit the employer to reduce or cancel unvested deferred awards before vesting (financial restatement, misconduct, risk management failure, significant downturn in financial performance). Require at least 50% of deferred variable remuneration to be in instruments (shares, share-linked instruments, or equivalent non-cash instruments). Specify the retention period post-vesting (typically 12 months for shares). Address the interaction with clawback provisions for amounts that have already vested and been paid.

variations.regulatory-compliant: Deferral structure compliant with PRA/FCA Remuneration Code for identified staff in regulated firms
variations.voluntary: Voluntary deferral arrangement with employer matching or enhanced vesting for deferred amounts
variations.performance-vesting: Deferred amounts vest subject to achievement of multi-year performance conditions

compliance_refs: EBA Guidelines on Sound Remuneration Policies (EBA/GL/2021/04)

clause_types.name: Intellectual Property Assignment
clause_types.description: Requires the employee to assign to the employer all intellectual property rights (patents, copyrights, designs, database rights, trade marks, and know-how) created by the employee in the course of employment or using the employer's resources. The clause must navigate the complex interaction between statutory provisions that automatically vest certain IP rights in the employer (such as UK Patents Act 1977 s.39 and UK CDPA 1988 s.11) and those that require express assignment.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Include a present assignment of all IP created in the course of employment ("hereby assigns" not "agrees to assign"). Specify the scope: IP created (i) in the course of the employee's normal duties, (ii) in the course of specifically assigned duties, (iii) using the employer's resources or confidential information, or (iv) that relates to the employer's business. Address moral rights: in the UK, require a waiver of moral rights under CDPA 1988 s.87 (note: moral rights cannot be assigned, only waived). Require the employee to disclose all potentially relevant IP created during employment. Include a power of attorney enabling the employer to execute IP registration documents on the employee's behalf if the employee is unavailable. Address pre-existing IP owned by the employee and specify the terms on which it is licensed to the employer if incorporated into work product. Consider the inventor compensation provisions under the Patents Act 1977 s.40 (outstanding benefit test). Specify that the assignment survives termination. Address the treatment of IP created using employer resources outside working hours.

variations.broad-assignment: Assignment of all IP related to the employer's current or anticipated business activities
variations.duty-limited: Assignment limited to IP created in the course of the employee's normal or specifically assigned duties
variations.with-inventor-compensation: Assignment with an express compensation mechanism for employee inventions of outstanding benefit

compliance_refs: UK Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 s.11, s.87

clause_types.name: Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure
clause_types.description: Imposes obligations on the employee to protect the employer's confidential information and trade secrets during and after the employment relationship. While employees owe an implied duty of confidentiality during employment under common law, the scope of protection narrows significantly after termination to cover only true trade secrets and information of a sufficiently high degree of confidentiality. An express confidentiality clause is therefore essential to define and extend protection beyond what common law provides.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define confidential information broadly but with specificity, including: trade secrets, business plans, financial information, client lists, pricing data, algorithms, source code, technical know-how, marketing strategies, and personnel information. Exclude information that is or becomes publicly available (other than through the employee's breach), information the employee can demonstrate they possessed before employment, and information received from a third party without restriction. Specify the duration of the confidentiality obligation post-termination (indefinite for true trade secrets; 2-5 years for other confidential information). Address the permitted disclosures: legal requirement, regulatory obligation, whistleblowing. Include the obligation to return or destroy all confidential information upon termination. Reference the distinction between Faccenda Chicken Class 1, 2, and 3 information under English law. Address the interaction with the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 and EU Trade Secrets Directive 2016/943. Specify remedies for breach (injunctive relief, damages, account of profits).

variations.comprehensive: Broad definition of confidential information with indefinite post-termination duration for trade secrets
variations.time-limited: Confidentiality obligation with a specified expiry date post-termination (typically 2-5 years)
variations.tiered: Different levels of protection and duration for different categories of information (trade secrets, confidential, restricted)

compliance_refs: UK case law: Faccenda Chicken Ltd v Fowler [1987] Ch 117

clause_types.name: Whistleblower Protection
clause_types.description: Establishes protections for employees who report breaches of law, regulatory requirements, or internal policies through designated reporting channels, in compliance with the EU Whistleblower Protection Directive 2019/1937 and implementing national legislation. The clause must ensure that reporting persons are protected from retaliation, that confidentiality of their identity is maintained, and that reports are investigated through a fair and impartial process.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the scope of protected disclosures (breaches of EU law in areas listed in Directive 2019/1937 Art 2: public procurement, financial services, product safety, transport safety, environmental protection, radiation protection, food safety, public health, consumer protection, data protection, and other areas). Define the internal reporting channels (dedicated reporting platform, compliance officer, ombudsperson, ethics hotline). Specify the procedural requirements: acknowledgement of receipt within seven days, feedback to the reporting person within three months, impartial investigation, documented outcome. Prohibit all forms of retaliation (dismissal, demotion, withholding of training, negative performance assessment, coercion, intimidation, harassment). Establish a reversal of the burden of proof for retaliation claims (where the reporting person establishes a prima facie case of detriment, the employer must prove the measure was based on duly justified grounds). Address anonymous reporting and the protection of the reporter's identity. Specify the protection for facilitators (persons who assist the reporting person) and third parties connected to the reporting person.

variations.directive-compliant: Full compliance with EU Whistleblower Protection Directive 2019/1937 including all mandatory safeguards
variations.enhanced: Extended scope beyond EU Directive requirements to cover all internal policy violations and ethical concerns
variations.multi-channel: Multiple reporting channels including internal hotline, external ombudsperson, and digital platform

clause_types.name: Employee Data Protection and Subject Access
clause_types.description: Addresses the employer's obligations regarding the collection, processing, and retention of employee personal data under the GDPR and applicable national data protection legislation, including the employee's rights as a data subject. Employment relationships involve extensive processing of sensitive personal data (health data, biometric data, financial data, performance data), requiring a clear lawful basis and transparency regarding the purposes and scope of processing.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the categories of personal data processed (identity, contact, financial, health, performance, disciplinary, monitoring data). Identify the lawful basis for each category of processing (contract performance under Art 6(1)(b), legitimate interest under Art 6(1)(f), legal obligation under Art 6(1)(c), or explicit consent under Art 9(2)(a) for special category data). Provide a clear privacy notice compliant with GDPR Art 13-14. Address the employee's data subject rights: access (Art 15), rectification (Art 16), erasure (Art 17), restriction (Art 18), portability (Art 20), and objection (Art 21). Specify the retention periods for each category of employee data. Address cross-border data transfers (where employer has entities outside the EEA). Address workplace monitoring (email, internet, CCTV, GPS tracking) with reference to WP29 Opinion 2/2017 and EDPB Guidelines. Specify the process for handling data subject access requests from employees. Address automated decision-making in HR processes (recruitment screening, performance scoring) under Art 22.

variations.gdpr-standard: Standard GDPR-compliant employee data processing provisions with full privacy notice
variations.monitoring-enhanced: Enhanced provisions addressing workplace monitoring, including email, internet, CCTV, and device monitoring
variations.international: Provisions addressing cross-border data transfers within a multinational group, including Standard Contractual Clauses

compliance_refs: EDPB Guidelines on processing personal data in the context of connected vehicles
WP29 Opinion 2/2017 on data processing at work

clause_types.name: Grievance Procedure
clause_types.description: Establishes a formal internal procedure for employees to raise concerns, complaints, or disputes regarding their employment conditions, treatment by colleagues or management, or any other workplace matter. A well-drafted grievance procedure promotes early resolution of disputes, reduces tribunal claims, and demonstrates the employer's commitment to fair treatment. Under the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures, failure to follow a proper procedure may result in an uplift of up to 25% on tribunal awards.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Structure the procedure in clear stages: informal resolution (discussion with line manager), formal stage 1 (written grievance to designated manager), formal stage 2 (appeal to a more senior manager or panel). Specify the time limits for each stage (typically 5 working days for acknowledgement, 10-15 working days for investigation and hearing). Require the employee to set out the grievance in writing, identifying the issues and the desired resolution. Guarantee the right to be accompanied by a trade union representative or work colleague at formal grievance hearings (UK Employment Relations Act 1999 s.10). Specify the investigation process: appointment of an impartial investigator, gathering of evidence, interviews with relevant parties. Address grievances raised during disciplinary proceedings and the appropriate sequencing. Specify the documentation requirements (written outcome letter, right of appeal, records retention). Address collective grievances and the interface with trade union consultation. Ensure compliance with the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures.

variations.acas-aligned: Procedure fully aligned with the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures
variations.mediation-integrated: Procedure incorporating a voluntary mediation stage before escalation to formal grievance
variations.multi-stage: Extended multi-stage process with separate panels for first-instance hearing and appeal

compliance_refs: ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures

clause_types.name: Disciplinary Procedure
clause_types.description: Establishes the employer's formal procedure for addressing employee misconduct, poor performance, or other breaches of employment terms, including the investigation process, hearing rights, sanctions, and appeal mechanism. A fair and well-documented disciplinary procedure is essential for defending unfair dismissal claims and must be consistent with the ACAS Code of Practice, which tribunals are required to take into account when considering relevant cases.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the categories of misconduct subject to the disciplinary procedure: minor misconduct (lateness, dress code violations), serious misconduct (insubordination, negligence, breach of policy), and gross misconduct (fraud, theft, violence, serious breach of confidentiality, harassment). Specify the investigation process: appointment of an investigating officer separate from the decision-maker, gathering of documentary and witness evidence, opportunity for the employee to respond. Specify the hearing process: written notice of the allegations and evidence, right to be accompanied by a trade union representative or colleague, right to present evidence and call witnesses, and right to respond to allegations. Define the available sanctions: verbal warning, written warning, final written warning, demotion, dismissal with notice, summary dismissal without notice (for gross misconduct). Specify the appeal process: right to appeal within a specified period (typically 5 working days), appeal heard by a more senior manager not involved in the original decision. Specify the duration of warnings on file (typically 6 months for verbal warnings, 12 months for written warnings). Address suspension on full pay pending investigation.

variations.acas-code: Procedure strictly following the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures
variations.fast-track: Expedited procedure for cases of gross misconduct permitting summary dismissal following investigation and hearing
variations.performance-specific: Separate performance improvement process with capability review, performance improvement plan (PIP), and defined assessment periods

compliance_refs: ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures

clause_types.name: Health and Safety Obligations
clause_types.description: Defines the mutual obligations of the employer and employee regarding workplace health and safety, including the employer's duty to provide a safe working environment and safe systems of work, and the employee's duty to comply with health and safety policies and cooperate with the employer's safety measures. The clause must address the employer's obligations under the EU Framework Directive 89/391/EEC and national implementing legislation.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the employer's obligations: provision of a safe workplace, risk assessments, safe systems of work, information and training, personal protective equipment, health surveillance where required. Specify the employee's obligations: compliance with health and safety policies, use of safety equipment, reporting of hazards and incidents, cooperation with the employer's health and safety measures. Address the requirement for risk assessments (general and specific: DSE, manual handling, hazardous substances, pregnant workers, young workers). Specify the obligations regarding workplace accidents and incidents: immediate reporting, investigation, RIDDOR reporting (UK) or equivalent national reporting requirements. Address the right of employees to stop work in the event of serious and imminent danger without suffering detriment (EU Framework Directive Art 8(4)). Specify the employer's obligations regarding mental health and psychosocial risks. Address the obligations regarding home workers and remote workers. Specify the consequences of employee non-compliance with health and safety requirements (disciplinary action).

variations.office-based: Health and safety provisions focused on office environment risks (DSE, ergonomics, fire safety, mental health)
variations.industrial: Comprehensive provisions for industrial or manufacturing environments including machinery safety, hazardous substances, and PPE
variations.hybrid: Provisions addressing both on-site and remote working health and safety obligations

compliance_refs: UK Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
UK Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999

clause_types.name: Remote and Hybrid Working Arrangements
clause_types.description: Establishes the terms and conditions for remote or hybrid working arrangements, including eligibility, working hours, equipment provision, expense reimbursement, data security, health and safety in the home workspace, and the employer's right to require attendance at the workplace. The EU Framework Agreement on Telework and national implementing legislation in many EU Member States impose specific obligations on employers providing remote work arrangements.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify whether remote working is a contractual right or a discretionary arrangement that may be withdrawn with notice. Define the hybrid working pattern (e.g., minimum days in the office per week or month). Specify the employer's obligations: provision of equipment (laptop, monitor, keyboard, chair), contribution to home office costs (internet, electricity, heating), maintenance and technical support. Specify the employee's obligations: maintenance of a suitable workspace, compliance with data security policies (encrypted devices, secure network, screen lock), availability during core hours, attendance at mandatory in-person events. Address health and safety in the home workspace: requirement for a DSE self-assessment, employer's right to inspect or request photographs of the workspace, provision of ergonomic equipment. Address data protection and information security: prohibition on use of public WiFi, secure storage of confidential documents, compliance with the employer's ISMS policies. Specify the right to disconnect and working time recording obligations. Address the tax implications of home working (P11D benefits, home office allowance). Specify the applicable jurisdiction and governing law where remote working crosses national borders.

variations.fully-remote: Permanent remote working arrangement with no regular office attendance requirement
variations.hybrid-structured: Structured hybrid arrangement with specified office and remote days each week
variations.discretionary: Remote working at the employer's discretion, revocable with reasonable notice

compliance_refs: UK Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 s.2

clause_types.name: Outside Activities and Secondary Employment
clause_types.description: Regulates the employee's ability to engage in secondary employment, directorships, consultancy work, or other outside activities during the term of employment. Under EU Directive 2019/1152 on Transparent and Predictable Working Conditions, Member States must ensure that employers do not prohibit workers from taking up employment with other employers outside the work schedule established with that employer, except where restrictions are justified on the grounds of health and safety, protection of business confidentiality, integrity of the public service, or avoidance of conflicts of interest.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify whether outside activities require prior written consent from the employer or merely prior notification. Define the categories of activity subject to the restriction: paid employment, directorships, consultancy, voluntary work, political office. Avoid blanket prohibitions that would violate EU Directive 2019/1152 Art 9(1); instead, specify the legitimate grounds for restriction (conflict of interest, Working Time Directive compliance, protection of confidential information, reputational risk). Require the employee to disclose any existing outside activities at the commencement of employment. Specify the process for requesting consent: written application, assessment criteria (conflict of interest, time commitment, reputational impact), response timeline. Address the interaction with the Working Time Directive 2003/88/EC and the aggregate weekly working time limit of 48 hours (opt-out where applicable). Specify the consequences of engaging in unauthorized outside activities (disciplinary action). Include a carve-out for de minimis activities (charitable work, personal investments, academic publishing).

variations.consent-required: All outside employment and directorships require prior written consent from a designated authority
variations.notification-only: Employee must notify the employer of outside activities but consent is not required unless a conflict arises
variations.restricted-categories: Prohibition on specific categories of outside activity (competing businesses, directorship of regulated entities) with freedom for other activities

clause_types.name: Post-Termination Obligations
clause_types.description: Consolidates all obligations that survive the termination of the employment relationship, including the return of company property and information, cooperation with ongoing matters, handover obligations, and the continued observance of confidentiality, non-compete, and non-solicitation restrictions. A well-drafted post-termination clause ensures an orderly transition and reduces the risk of disputes during the vulnerable period following the employee's departure.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the obligations that arise immediately upon termination: return of all company property (laptop, phone, access cards, vehicles, documents, data), deletion of company data from personal devices, resignation from any directorships or offices held on behalf of the employer. Define the handover obligations: preparation of a handover document, briefing of successors, completion of pending matters within the employee's control. Specify cooperation obligations: availability to answer questions from the successor (for a reasonable period, typically 3-6 months), assistance with litigation or regulatory investigations in which the employee's evidence is required (with reasonable compensation for time). List the surviving obligations with their respective durations: confidentiality (indefinite for trade secrets, specified period for other information), non-compete (specified months), non-solicitation (specified months). Address the employer's obligation to provide a reference and any agreed form of reference. Specify the final payment obligations: accrued salary, accrued holiday pay, pro-rata bonus (if applicable), expenses, and the timing of final payment. Address the consequences of failure to return company property.

variations.comprehensive: Full suite of post-termination obligations including return of property, handover, cooperation, and surviving restrictions
variations.executive: Enhanced post-termination obligations for senior executives including agreed reference, announcements, and transition support
variations.minimal: Basic post-termination obligations limited to return of property and confidentiality

compliance_refs: GDPR Art 17 (right to erasure of employee data)

clause_types.name: Severance and Termination Payments
clause_types.description: Defines the payments and benefits payable to the employee upon termination of employment, beyond statutory minimum entitlements. Severance provisions typically cover payment in lieu of notice (PILON), enhanced redundancy payments, pro-rata bonus, accelerated equity vesting, continuation of benefits, and outplacement support. The tax treatment of severance payments must be carefully structured to take advantage of the GBP 30,000 exemption under UK tax law or equivalent exemptions in other jurisdictions.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the components of the severance package: payment in lieu of notice (PILON), enhanced redundancy payment (multiple of statutory or contractual notice pay), pro-rata bonus for the current performance year, treatment of deferred compensation and unvested equity, continuation of benefits (health insurance, life insurance) for a specified period post-termination, outplacement support. Include an express PILON clause to ensure tax-efficient treatment (a contractual right to make PILON means the payment is taxable as earnings rather than as a termination payment eligible for the GBP 30,000 exemption). Consider structuring the severance to maximise the GBP 30,000 tax exemption under ITEPA 2003 s.401-404. Address the conditions for severance eligibility (typically not available on termination for gross misconduct). Specify the payment terms (lump sum or instalments) and whether severance is subject to mitigation (reduction if the employee obtains alternative employment). Address the interaction with settlement agreements and the requirement for independent legal advice.

variations.enhanced-redundancy: Enhanced redundancy payment based on a multiple of statutory redundancy pay, typically 1-2 weeks per year of service
variations.executive-package: Comprehensive executive severance including PILON, enhanced redundancy, accelerated vesting, benefits continuation, and outplacement
variations.contractual-pilon: Payment in lieu of notice as a contractual entitlement, enabling clean break termination

compliance_refs: UK Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003 s.401-404

clause_types.name: Relocation Clause
clause_types.description: Governs the terms under which the employer may require the employee to relocate to a different work location, or under which the employer provides relocation assistance when the employee is hired from another geographic area. The clause addresses mobility obligations, relocation expenses, temporary accommodation, spousal support, and clawback of relocation costs if the employee leaves within a specified period.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Distinguish between (i) a mobility clause permitting the employer to require relocation and (ii) a relocation assistance package offered as part of the hiring terms. For mobility clauses, specify the geographic scope (within the same city, within the country, internationally), the notice period for relocation (typically 3-6 months), and the reasonableness test that will be applied. Address the right to refuse relocation and the consequences (potential redundancy situation). For relocation assistance, specify the components: removal costs, temporary accommodation (duration and budget), property search support, stamp duty or equivalent costs, spousal career support, school search assistance, tax equalisation for international relocations. Include a clawback provision for relocation costs if the employee voluntarily resigns within a specified period (typically 12-24 months), with the clawback reducing on a pro-rata basis. Address the tax treatment of relocation expenses and the employer's withholding obligations. For international relocations, address immigration and work permit requirements.

variations.domestic: Relocation assistance for domestic moves, covering removal costs, temporary accommodation, and property transaction costs
variations.international: Comprehensive international relocation package including immigration support, tax equalisation, spousal assistance, and schooling
variations.mobility-clause: Employer's right to require relocation with reasonable notice and defined geographic scope

compliance_refs: UK Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003 s.271-289 (relocation expenses exemption)

clause_types.name: Training Cost Repayment
clause_types.description: Requires the employee to repay the costs of employer-funded training, qualifications, or professional development if the employee leaves employment within a specified period following completion of the training. Training repayment clauses must be carefully drafted to be enforceable, as courts will scrutinise whether the repayment obligation is a genuine pre-estimate of the employer's loss or constitutes a penalty or unreasonable restraint of trade.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the types of training subject to repayment (professional qualifications, external courses, certifications, degree programmes, but typically not mandatory training required by the employer for the role). Define the repayment period (typically 12-24 months from completion of the training, with the obligation reducing on a sliding scale). Specify the repayment amount: full cost during the first period, reducing on a monthly or quarterly pro-rata basis thereafter. Define what constitutes the cost (course fees, examination fees, study materials, travel, study leave salary). Specify the trigger for repayment (voluntary resignation or dismissal for gross misconduct within the repayment period). Address the deduction mechanism and compliance with Part II of the Employment Rights Act 1996 (requirement for written authorisation of deductions from wages). Ensure the clause does not operate as a penalty or unreasonable restraint of trade (Neil v Strathclyde Regional Council [1984]). Consider whether the clause is reasonable in the context of the employee's salary level.

variations.sliding-scale: Repayment obligation reduces on a monthly sliding scale over 12-24 months
variations.full-recovery: Full cost recovery within 12 months, reducing to 50% in months 13-24
variations.conditional: Repayment only triggered if the employee voluntarily resigns; no repayment obligation if dismissed by the employer

compliance_refs: UK Employment Rights Act 1996 s.13 (deductions from wages)
UK case law: Neil v Strathclyde Regional Council [1984]

clause_types.name: Retirement and Pension Benefits
clause_types.description: Specifies the employer's pension and retirement benefit obligations, including the type of pension scheme (defined benefit or defined contribution), contribution rates, eligibility criteria, and the interaction with statutory auto-enrolment requirements. Pension provisions represent a significant financial commitment and must comply with complex regulatory requirements including the Pensions Act 2008 (auto-enrolment), the Pensions Act 2004, and, for cross-border arrangements, the IORP II Directive.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the type of pension arrangement: defined contribution (employer and employee contribution rates), defined benefit (accrual rate, pensionable salary definition, normal retirement age), or hybrid. For defined contribution schemes, specify the employer's contribution rate (as a percentage of pensionable salary), the employee's contribution rate, and salary sacrifice options. Ensure compliance with auto-enrolment requirements under the Pensions Act 2008 (minimum employer contribution currently 3% of qualifying earnings; total minimum contribution 8%). Specify the definition of pensionable salary (basic salary only, or including allowances and bonuses). Address the employer's right to substitute the pension scheme or amend contribution rates (subject to consultation). For senior executives, consider supplementary pension arrangements (funded or unfunded supplementary retirement benefits, employer-financed retirement benefit scheme). Address the interaction with lifetime allowance, annual allowance, and salary sacrifice arrangements. Specify the death-in-service benefit (typically 4x basic salary). Address the treatment of pension contributions during maternity, paternity, and sick leave.

variations.defined-contribution: Employer-matched defined contribution pension with specified contribution rates
variations.executive-supplementary: Enhanced pension arrangement for senior executives, potentially including supplementary unfunded retirement benefits
variations.salary-sacrifice: Pension contributions structured through a salary sacrifice arrangement for National Insurance efficiency

compliance_refs: UK Finance Act (annual allowance and lifetime allowance provisions)

clause_types.name: Parental and Family Leave
clause_types.description: Establishes the employee's entitlements to maternity leave, paternity leave, shared parental leave, adoption leave, parental leave, and other family-related leave, including the applicable pay, benefits continuation, and return-to-work rights. The clause must comply with the EU Work-Life Balance Directive 2019/1158 and applicable national implementing legislation, while many employers offer enhanced entitlements beyond the statutory minimum as part of their benefits package.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the entitlement to each type of family leave: maternity leave (duration, pay, qualification criteria), paternity leave (duration, pay), shared parental leave (mechanism for sharing leave between parents), adoption leave, parental leave, time off for dependants. For enhanced maternity pay, specify the duration and amount (e.g., 26 weeks at full pay followed by statutory maternity pay). Specify the qualifying criteria for enhanced pay (typically minimum 26 weeks' continuous service). Address the accrual of holiday entitlement during family leave. Specify the continuation of benefits during leave (pension contributions, life insurance, health insurance, company car). Address the right to return to the same or a suitable alternative role. Under the EU Work-Life Balance Directive 2019/1158, specify the two months of non-transferable parental leave for each parent, paid at a level determined by the Member State. Address the right to request flexible working upon return from parental leave. Specify the protection from detrimental treatment or dismissal connected to the exercise of family leave rights. Address keeping-in-touch (KIT) days during maternity and adoption leave.

variations.enhanced-maternity: Enhanced maternity pay beyond statutory minimum, typically 13-26 weeks at full pay
variations.equal-parental: Gender-neutral parental leave policy offering equal enhanced leave entitlements to all parents regardless of gender
variations.statutory-minimum: Leave entitlements at statutory minimum levels with no enhanced pay

compliance_refs: UK Maternity and Parental Leave etc. Regulations 1999

clause_types.name: Flexible Working Arrangements
clause_types.description: Establishes the framework for employees to request and, where agreed, work under flexible working arrangements including part-time work, compressed hours, job sharing, flexitime, staggered hours, and annualised hours. Under the UK Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023, all employees have the right to request flexible working from day one of employment, and employers must deal with requests in a reasonable manner within a two-month decision period.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the types of flexible working available (part-time, compressed hours, flexitime, job sharing, staggered hours, annualised hours, term-time working). Define the application process in compliance with the Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023: right to make two statutory requests per year, employer must respond within two months, employer may refuse only on specified business grounds. List the permitted grounds for refusal (burden of additional costs, detrimental effect on ability to meet customer demand, inability to reorganise work among existing staff, inability to recruit additional staff, detrimental impact on quality or performance, insufficiency of work during the periods the employee proposes to work, planned structural changes). Specify the trial period mechanism for flexible working arrangements. Address the right to revert to original working pattern. Specify the impact of flexible working on pro-rata salary, holiday entitlement, pension contributions, and other benefits. Address the interaction with remote working provisions. Ensure that the process does not discriminate indirectly on the grounds of sex or disability.

variations.statutory-compliant: Process aligned with the Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 statutory requirements
variations.enhanced: Employer proactively offers flexible working options at recruitment and during employment, beyond statutory requirements
variations.hybrid-framework: Structured hybrid working framework combining office and remote working with flexible core hours

clause_types.name: Equal Pay and Pay Transparency
clause_types.description: Addresses the employer's obligation to provide equal pay for equal work or work of equal value regardless of gender, and the emerging requirements for pay transparency under the EU Pay Transparency Directive 2023/970. The clause establishes mechanisms for pay audits, pay gap reporting, and remediation of unjustified pay disparities, and specifies the employee's right to information about pay levels and criteria.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Affirm the employer's commitment to equal pay for equal work or work of equal value in compliance with Article 157 TFEU and the UK Equality Act 2010 s.64-80. Address the requirements of the EU Pay Transparency Directive 2023/970 (to be transposed by June 2026): right of applicants to receive information on pay range before interview, prohibition on asking salary history, right of employees to information on average pay levels by gender for equivalent work, and mandatory reporting of gender pay gap data. Specify the job evaluation methodology used to determine work of equal value. Require regular pay audits (at least every three years for employers with 100+ employees under the EU Directive). Address the remediation process for identified pay gaps: analysis of whether pay differences are justified by objective, gender-neutral criteria, and corrective action timeline. Specify the employer's reporting obligations (UK gender pay gap reporting for employers with 250+ employees). Address the interaction with the right to discuss pay with colleagues (prohibition on pay secrecy clauses under EU Directive Art 7). Include provisions for ongoing monitoring and the role of employee representatives.

variations.eu-directive-compliant: Full compliance with EU Pay Transparency Directive 2023/970 including pay range disclosure, reporting, and audit
variations.uk-reporting: Compliance with UK mandatory gender pay gap reporting requirements for employers with 250+ employees
variations.comprehensive: Enhanced pay transparency framework exceeding statutory requirements, including pay band publication and regular equality audits

compliance_refs: UK Equality Act 2010 (Gender Pay Gap Information) Regulations 2017

clause_types.name: Redundancy Provisions
clause_types.description: Establishes the substantive and procedural requirements for making employees redundant, including the definition of redundancy, selection criteria, consultation obligations, enhanced redundancy payments, and the right to time off for job-seeking. Redundancy procedures must comply with the individual and collective consultation requirements under national implementing legislation of the EU Collective Redundancies Directive 98/59/EC.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define redundancy in accordance with the statutory definition (UK Employment Rights Act 1996 s.139: cessation of business, closure of workplace, or diminished requirements for work of a particular kind). Specify the consultation obligations: individual consultation with each affected employee, and collective consultation with employee representatives where 20 or more redundancies are proposed within 90 days (30 days' consultation for 20-99 redundancies; 45 days for 100+ redundancies under UK TULRCA 1992 s.188). Define the selection criteria: objective, measurable, and non-discriminatory (skills, qualifications, attendance records, disciplinary records, performance assessments, length of service). Specify the enhanced redundancy payment: formula (e.g., one month's salary per year of service, capped at a maximum number of years) or a fixed multiple of statutory redundancy pay. Specify alternative employment obligations: duty to offer suitable alternative employment, trial period in new role. Address the right to time off for job-seeking (UK Employment Rights Act 1996 s.52). Specify the notification to the Redundancy Payments Service (HR1 form). Address the interaction with TUPE provisions where redundancy follows a transfer.

variations.enhanced-payment: Enhanced redundancy payment above statutory minimum, typically based on actual salary with no statutory cap
variations.voluntary-first: Voluntary redundancy scheme offered before compulsory selection, often with enhanced terms
variations.collective-consultation: Comprehensive collective consultation framework with elected or nominated employee representatives

compliance_refs: UK Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992 s.188-198

clause_types.name: Change of Control and Retention
clause_types.description: Provides for enhanced severance, accelerated equity vesting, retention bonuses, or other protections for key employees in the event of a change of control of the employer (acquisition, merger, or significant change in ownership). Change of control provisions are critical for retaining key talent during M&A transactions and for protecting executives from termination following a takeover.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the change of control trigger events: acquisition of a specified percentage of voting shares (typically 50% or more), sale of all or substantially all assets, merger or consolidation, change in the composition of the board of directors. Specify the protections available: single-trigger benefits (payable upon the change of control event itself) or double-trigger benefits (payable only upon the change of control plus subsequent termination or constructive dismissal within a specified period, typically 12-24 months). For retention bonuses, specify the retention period (typically 12-24 months from the change of control), the payment schedule (50% on commencement, 50% at the end of the retention period), and the clawback conditions. For accelerated equity vesting, specify whether acceleration is full or partial and whether it applies to time-based and performance-based awards. Address the definition of good reason (constructive dismissal triggers): material reduction in role, salary, or reporting level; relocation beyond a specified distance; material breach of employment terms. Specify the tax treatment of change of control payments, including the potential application of excess parachute payment rules.

variations.single-trigger: Benefits payable automatically upon change of control, regardless of whether the employee is terminated
variations.double-trigger: Benefits payable only upon change of control followed by termination or constructive dismissal within a specified period
variations.retention-bonus: Cash retention bonus payable for remaining in employment through and after the change of control transition period

compliance_refs: UK City Code on Takeovers and Mergers (Rule 16)

clause_types.name: Discretionary Bonus
clause_types.description: Establishes the terms of the employer's discretionary bonus scheme, including the performance assessment criteria, the employer's discretion in determining bonus amounts, and the conditions that must be satisfied for bonus eligibility. Courts have increasingly scrutinised discretionary bonus provisions, requiring employers to exercise their discretion rationally and in good faith, and have been willing to imply obligations of transparency and non-arbitrariness into bonus schemes.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify that the bonus is discretionary and that there is no contractual entitlement to a bonus in any year (but recognise that repeated payment may give rise to a contractual entitlement through custom and practice). Define the performance assessment criteria: individual performance (KPIs, competency assessment), team performance, business unit performance, and company financial performance. Specify the performance period and assessment timeline. Address the employer's discretion to determine the bonus pool and individual allocations, subject to the duty to exercise discretion rationally, in good faith, and not in an arbitrary, capricious, or perverse manner (Braganza v BP Shipping Ltd [2015] UKSC 17). Specify the payment conditions: the employee must be in employment and not under notice on the payment date (but note that this condition may be unenforceable for employees terminated during the performance period). Address the pro-rating of bonus for employees who join or leave during the performance period. Specify the deferral and malus/clawback provisions for variable remuneration (link to emp-bonus-deferral and emp-clawback). Address the tax treatment and employer's NIC obligations.

variations.wholly-discretionary: Bonus entirely at the employer's discretion with no guaranteed minimum or formula
variations.formulaic: Bonus calculated by reference to a defined formula based on measurable performance metrics, with limited discretion
variations.target-based: Bonus linked to achievement of specified targets with a target bonus percentage and maximum cap

compliance_refs: UK case law: Braganza v BP Shipping Ltd [2015] UKSC 17
UK case law: Keen v Commerzbank AG [2006] EWCA Civ 1536

clause_types.name: Commission Structure
clause_types.description: Defines the commission-based compensation structure for sales, business development, or revenue-generating employees, including the commission rates, qualifying transactions, measurement period, payment timing, and treatment of commissions upon termination. Commission structures must be drafted with precision to avoid disputes over entitlement, particularly regarding deals in the pipeline at the date of termination and post-termination commissions on transactions initiated by the departing employee.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the qualifying transactions or events that generate commission entitlement (signed contracts, delivered revenue, collected cash, or a combination). Specify the commission rate(s): flat rate, tiered (increasing with volume), or regressive (decreasing beyond quota). Define the measurement period (monthly, quarterly, annually). Specify the commission base: gross revenue, net revenue, gross margin, or contract value. Address the treatment of multi-year contracts (commission on the full contract value or annual revenue). Specify draws against commission and the reconciliation mechanism. Address accelerators and decelerators tied to quota attainment. Specify the timing of commission payment (typically within 30 days of the qualifying event). Address the treatment of returned, cancelled, or disputed transactions (commission clawback). Critically, define the treatment of commissions upon termination: entitlement to commissions on deals closed before termination, treatment of pipeline deals, and any post-termination commission tail. Specify the right to amend the commission plan and the notice period for changes.

variations.revenue-based: Commission calculated as a percentage of recognised revenue from qualifying transactions
variations.margin-based: Commission calculated as a percentage of gross margin or gross profit on qualifying transactions
variations.tiered-accelerator: Tiered commission rates with accelerators for above-quota performance and potential decelerators for underperformance

compliance_refs: UK Employment Rights Act 1996 s.13 (deductions from wages)

clause_types.name: Expense Reimbursement Policy
clause_types.description: Establishes the employer's obligations and procedures for reimbursing employees for legitimate business expenses incurred in the performance of their duties, including travel, accommodation, subsistence, entertainment, and other business-related costs. The expense policy must balance the employer's duty to reimburse reasonable expenses with the need for fiscal control and compliance with tax requirements.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the categories of reimbursable expenses: travel (public transport, mileage, flights, taxis), accommodation, meals and subsistence, client entertainment, professional subscriptions, working-from-home expenses. Define the approval process: pre-approval for expenses above a specified threshold, line manager authorisation, submission deadlines. Specify the per diem rates or maximum amounts for each category. Address the documentation requirements: original receipts, itemised invoices, business justification. Specify the payment mechanism and timeline (reimbursement through payroll or expense management system within a specified period). Address company credit card use and obligations. Specify the consequences of fraudulent or non-compliant expense claims (disciplinary action, recovery of overpayments). Address the tax treatment of expenses: tax-exempt payments for qualifying business expenses under ITEPA 2003 s.336-339, dispensation agreements, and PAYE Settlement Agreements for residual benefits. Consider the use of benchmark scale rates approved by HMRC.

variations.reimbursement: Employee pays expenses personally and claims reimbursement with supporting documentation
variations.company-card: Expenses charged to a company credit card with monthly reconciliation and approval
variations.per-diem: Fixed per diem rates for travel, accommodation, and subsistence, eliminating the need for individual receipts

compliance_refs: UK Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003 s.336-339

clause_types.name: Company Property and Equipment
clause_types.description: Governs the provision, use, care, and return of company-owned property and equipment issued to the employee for the performance of their duties, including IT equipment (laptops, mobile phones, monitors), vehicles, access cards, and other assets. The clause must address the employee's obligations regarding care and appropriate use of company property, data security, and the consequences of loss, damage, or failure to return property upon termination.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the items of company property provided (laptop, mobile phone, monitor, keyboard, mouse, company car, fuel card, access cards, keys). Define the acceptable use: business purposes only, or permitted limited personal use (specify any restrictions). Specify the employee's obligations: reasonable care, compliance with IT security policies (encryption, password protection, software updates), prohibition on installing unauthorised software, obligation to report loss, theft, or damage immediately. Address the data security requirements: prohibition on storing company data on personal devices (unless authorised under a BYOD policy), obligation to use VPN for remote access, prohibition on disabling security features. Specify the return obligations upon termination: all items to be returned by the last day of employment or within a specified period. Address the consequences of failure to return property: the employer's right to deduct the replacement cost from final salary (subject to written authorisation under Employment Rights Act 1996 s.13). Specify the right of the employer to remotely wipe company data from devices upon termination. Address the tax treatment of equipment provided (benefit in kind under P11D, or trivial benefits exemption).

variations.standard-issue: Standard equipment provision with return obligations and acceptable use policy
variations.byod: Bring-your-own-device policy with employer contribution, data separation requirements, and remote wipe capability
variations.executive: Enhanced equipment provision for senior executives including company vehicle, mobile phone, and home office setup

compliance_refs: UK Employment Rights Act 1996 s.13 (deductions from wages)

clause_types.name: Social Media and Online Conduct Policy
clause_types.description: Regulates the employee's use of social media platforms and online conduct, both during and outside working hours, to protect the employer's reputation, confidential information, and client relationships. The policy must balance the employer's legitimate interests with the employee's rights to freedom of expression and privacy, and must comply with data protection legislation regarding the monitoring of employees' online activity.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Define the scope of social media covered (LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, personal blogs, online forums, and any future platforms). Distinguish between professional use of social media (on behalf of the employer) and personal use. For professional use, specify approval requirements, brand guidelines, and the process for managing official company accounts. For personal use, specify the restrictions: prohibition on disclosing confidential information, prohibition on making defamatory or discriminatory statements about the employer, colleagues, clients, or competitors, requirement to include a disclaimer that views are personal and do not represent the employer. Address the interaction between social media use and existing obligations (confidentiality, non-solicitation, non-compete). Specify the employer's monitoring of social media: extent, lawful basis under GDPR, and employee notification. Address the consequences of policy breaches (disciplinary action, up to and including summary dismissal for serious breaches). Specify the protection for whistleblowing disclosures made through social media. Address the ownership and handover of professional social media accounts and connections upon termination.

variations.liberal: Permissive approach with minimal restrictions on personal social media use, focusing on core prohibitions
variations.restrictive: Comprehensive restrictions on social media use with prior approval required for any employer-related content
variations.industry-specific: Tailored provisions for regulated industries (financial services, healthcare) with additional compliance requirements

compliance_refs: ECHR Art 8 (right to respect for private life)

clause_types.name: Drug and Alcohol Testing Policy
clause_types.description: Establishes the employer's policy for testing employees for drug and alcohol use, including the circumstances in which testing may be conducted, the testing procedures, the consequences of positive results, and the safeguards for employee rights. Drug and alcohol testing policies must be carefully balanced against the employee's right to privacy and must comply with data protection legislation, as test results constitute special category health data under the GDPR.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the circumstances in which testing may be conducted: pre-employment, random, for-cause (reasonable suspicion of impairment), post-incident, return-to-duty. Define the substances tested (alcohol, controlled drugs, prescription medications that may impair performance). Specify the testing methodology (urine, oral fluid, blood, breath) and the accredited laboratory standards (e.g., UKAS-accredited laboratory). Address the chain of custody procedures to ensure test integrity. Specify the consequences of a positive test: further investigation, occupational health referral, employee assistance programme (EAP), disciplinary action, and the distinction between dependent use (referral and support) and recreational use (disciplinary). Address the refusal to submit to testing and its consequences. Specify the GDPR lawful basis for processing test results (special category data under Art 9): consent, legal obligation, or substantial public interest (for safety-critical roles). Address the retention period for test results. Specify the right to a confirmatory re-test. Address reasonable adjustments for employees with substance use disorders under the Equality Act 2010.

variations.safety-critical: Mandatory random testing programme for safety-critical roles (transport, construction, machinery operation)
variations.for-cause-only: Testing permitted only where there is reasonable cause to suspect impairment or following a workplace incident
variations.supportive: Policy emphasising support and rehabilitation through EAP, with disciplinary action as a last resort

compliance_refs: UK Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 s.2-3

clause_types.name: Background Screening and Vetting
clause_types.description: Establishes the employer's right to conduct pre-employment and, where appropriate, periodic background checks on employees, including identity verification, right to work, criminal records, credit history, qualification verification, employment references, and enhanced security vetting for regulated or security-sensitive roles. Background checks must comply with data protection requirements and the principle of proportionality.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the types of background checks to be conducted: identity verification, right to work check (mandatory under UK Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006), basic or enhanced DBS check (for eligible roles under the Rehabilitation of Offenders Act 1974 (Exceptions) Order 1975), credit reference check (for financial services or roles involving financial responsibility), qualification and employment history verification, professional registration verification, social media screening. Specify the GDPR lawful basis for each type of check (legal obligation for right to work; legitimate interest or consent for other checks). Address the principle of proportionality: checks should be relevant to the role and not excessively intrusive. Specify the timing of checks (pre-employment, upon promotion to sensitive roles, periodic re-screening for regulated roles). Address the consequences of adverse findings: opportunity for the candidate or employee to explain, assessment of relevance to the role, and decision-making process. Specify the retention period for vetting records (aligned with the purpose and GDPR Art 5(1)(e)). Address enhanced vetting requirements for financial services (FCA fit and proper person test, Senior Managers and Certification Regime).

variations.basic: Standard pre-employment checks including identity, right to work, and employment references
variations.enhanced: Comprehensive screening including DBS, credit checks, qualification verification, and social media review
variations.regulated: Enhanced vetting for regulated financial services roles including FCA fitness and propriety assessment and SMCR compliance

compliance_refs: UK Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006

clause_types.name: Employment References
clause_types.description: Addresses the employer's obligations and policies regarding the provision and receipt of employment references, including the scope of information disclosed, the duty of care owed to both the subject and the recipient of the reference, and the agreed form of reference in settlement situations. While there is no general legal obligation to provide a reference under English law, employers who do provide references owe a duty of care to ensure they are true, accurate, and fair.
clause_types.drafting_guidance: Specify the employer's policy on providing references: comprehensive character and performance references, or factual references only (dates of employment, job title, salary). For comprehensive references, specify the scope of information to be included and the authorisation process. Address the duty of care under Spring v Guardian Assurance plc [1995] 2 AC 296: the reference must be true, accurate, and fair, and must not give a misleading impression by omission. Specify who is authorised to provide references on behalf of the employer. Address the employee's right to see the reference before it is sent (not legally required but best practice). For settlement agreements, include a mutually agreed form of reference as an annex. Address the protection of personal data in references under GDPR (subject access requests for references received are governed by a partial exemption). Specify the retention period for copies of references given. Address the regulatory obligations to provide references in certain sectors (financial services under SM&CR).

variations.factual-only: Reference limited to confirmation of dates of employment, job title, and reason for leaving
variations.comprehensive: Full character and performance reference covering skills, conduct, reason for leaving, and suitability for new role
variations.agreed-form: Mutually agreed form of reference annexed to a settlement agreement

compliance_refs: UK case law: Spring v Guardian Assurance plc [1995] 2 AC 296
GDPR Art 15 (subject access) and Schedule 2 Part 4 Para 24 (references exemption)
